deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 23-year-old man.
He's presenting with low back pain, stiffness, and left eye discomfort.
The back pain is worse at night and feels like a dull ache in the back and buttock area.
On physical exam, there's tenderness in the paravertebral muscles, iliac crest, and ischial tuberosity.
His eye is inflamed, and the pupil is constricted.
The back pain and stiffness make me think of a possible inflammatory or arthritic condition.
The fact that it's worse at night suggests it's probably not a muscle strain because those usually get better with rest.
Sacroiliitis is mentioned, which is inflammation of the sacroiliac joint.
That makes me think of ankylosing spondylitis, which is a type of arthritis that affects the sacroiliac joints and can cause pain and stiffness, especially in the lower back and buttocks.
The eye inflammation and constricted pupil are concerning.
Ankylosing spondylitis can sometimes involve the eyes, leading to conditions like iritis or uveitis.
The pupil being constricted might indicate some involvement of the oculomotor nerve or inflammation around the eye, which is common in spondylitis.

The physical exam findings of paravertebral muscle tenderness and tenderness at the iliac crest and ischial tuberosity also align with ankylosing spondylitis, as it affects the surrounding muscles and bones in that area.
So putting it all together, the combination of back pain, stiffness, sacroiliitis on imaging, and eye inflammation points towards ankylosing spondylitis.
I don't think it's something like a bacterial infection because there's no mention of fever or other systemic symptoms.
It's more likely a chronic inflammatory condition.
